scenarioName: Unstable Supraventricular Tachycardia — Synchronized Cardioversion with Procedural Sedation

scenarioStory.history: 45-year-old male was on the treadmill at his neighborhood gym when he suddenly felt his heart 'go off' — palpitations he describes as 'something jumped inside my chest' followed by a sustained very rapid heart rate. He sat down, but unlike his prior episodes, this one is not stopping on its own. Within 15 minutes he became diaphoretic, light-headed, developed substernal chest pressure rated 5/10, and once nearly fainted when trying to walk to the lobby. Patient has had two prior episodes diagnosed as PSVT in the past 4 years that resolved with vagal maneuvers. He is otherwise healthy, no known structural heart disease, no medications.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS arrives to find a 45-year-old male in clear distress: pale, diaphoretic, with substernal chest pressure and lightheadedness. Initial monitor connection shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 190 — supraventricular tachycardia. Importantly, his blood pressure is 78/52 with worsening mentation and chest pain — this is unstable SVT. The teaching point is the rapid pivot from any pharmacologic plan straight to synchronized cardioversion, with procedural sedation/analgesia for the responsive patient. → Procedural sedation → sync cardioversion: Crew explains the procedure. Procedural sedation given: fentanyl 85 mcg IV (85 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 85 mcg, max 100 mcg) for analgesia, plus midazolam 2 mg IV slow push for amnesia/anxiolysis (small dose given hemodynamic instability — half the typical max). Pre-oxygenated, monitored, and synchronized cardioversion delivered at biphasic 100 J. Rhythm converts to sinus rhythm at 86 bpm with prompt restoration of BP and resolution of chest pain. → Sinus rhythm; transport: Patient transported to cardiology-capable receiving hospital with continuous monitoring. Mentation clearing as sedation wears off. SpO2 and EtCO2 monitored continuously for opioid/benzo-induced respiratory depression. Watch for SVT recurrence en route. Bedside handoff includes initial rhythm, sedation given, joules delivered, conversion documented.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Sudden onset of palpitations 30 minutes ago at the gym, now with chest pressure, severe lightheadedness, near-syncope, and inability to stand without holding the wall.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS arrives to find a 45-year-old male in clear distress: pale, diaphoretic, with substernal chest pressure and lightheadedness. Initial monitor connection shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 190 — supraventricular tachycardia. Importantly, his blood pressure is 78/52 with worsening mentation and chest pain — this is unstable SVT. The teaching point is the rapid pivot from any pharmacologic plan straight to synchronized cardioversion, with procedural sedation/analgesia for the responsive patient.
relatedChecklist: Primary survey; assess stability — chest pain + AMS + hypotension = UNSTABLE
O2 NC → NRB titrate to SpO2 94–98%
Cardiac monitor; defib pads on (sync mode pre-armed); 12-lead EKG
IV access (large-bore proximal — for sedation + post-conversion meds)
Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia driver
Classify rhythm: regular narrow complex = SVT; unstable → cardiovert (skip pharmacology)

scenarioEvents.name: Procedural sedation → sync cardioversion
scenarioEvents.description: Crew explains the procedure. Procedural sedation given: fentanyl 85 mcg IV (85 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 85 mcg, max 100 mcg) for analgesia, plus midazolam 2 mg IV slow push for amnesia/anxiolysis (small dose given hemodynamic instability — half the typical max). Pre-oxygenated, monitored, and synchronized cardioversion delivered at biphasic 100 J. Rhythm converts to sinus rhythm at 86 bpm with prompt restoration of BP and resolution of chest pain.
relatedChecklist: Fentanyl 85 mcg IV (85 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) — analgesia
Midazolam 2 mg slow IV push — amnesia/anxiolysis (small dose given hypotension)
Allergies, BP, SpO2 confirmed; capnography on (capno predicts hypoventilation early)
Synchronized cardioversion 100 J biphasic; reassess pulse/rhythm post-shock
Post-shock: confirm rhythm, pulse, BP; serial reassessment of mentation and pain

scenarioEvents.name: Sinus rhythm; transport
scenarioEvents.description: Patient transported to cardiology-capable receiving hospital with continuous monitoring. Mentation clearing as sedation wears off. SpO2 and EtCO2 monitored continuously for opioid/benzo-induced respiratory depression. Watch for SVT recurrence en route. Bedside handoff includes initial rhythm, sedation given, joules delivered, conversion documented.
relatedChecklist: Continuous monitor for SVT recurrence; pads stay on; pre-arm sync mode
Continuous SpO2 + capnography during sedation wear-off; reassess pain/RR q5 min
Transport to cardiology-capable hospital; bedside handoff with strips + dose timeline

scenarioEvents.name: Cardioversion without sedation
scenarioEvents.description: Crew cardioverted without giving any procedural sedation or analgesia. The shock was effective and the rhythm converted to sinus, but the alert patient experienced the full pain of the shock. He yells, becomes anxious and tearful, and the experience leaves a lasting psychological imprint that may affect his willingness to seek care for future episodes. The protocol explicitly says 'For responsive patients, consider sedation and analgesia' — this is the moment to do it, not after.
relatedChecklist: Fentanyl 85 mcg IV NOW (85 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) for residual chest wall pain
Verbal reassurance; explain procedure and outcome; non-pharmacologic measures
Encourage transport for cardiology evaluation despite distress
Document plan: pre-shock sedation/analgesia in future cardioversions; QI moment
